core_concept: Global Trade and Interdependence: How Nations Exchange Goods, Capital, and Ideas
facts: A country has a comparative advantage when it can produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country, not just when it produces more total output.
A tariff is a tax on imported goods that makes them more expensive, so domestic consumers pay more for foreign products when a tariff is in place.
Global supply chains mean a single product, like a smartphone, uses materials and labor from many countries because each country specializes in a different step of production.

High-income countries tend to export advanced goods and services, while low-income countries often export raw materials or agricultural products, because their economies have different levels of capital and technology.

A trade agreement between two or more countries reduces or removes tariffs on specific goods, making it cheaper for those countries to buy from each other than from countries without such an agreement.
misconceptions: Students believe that if a country exports more than it imports, that country is always better off economically, but trade is about mutual benefit and both sides gain from exchanging goods they produce efficiently.

Students think that a tariff is a fee paid by the exporting country to the importing country, when in fact it is a tax paid by the importing country's consumers or importers, which raises the price of the imported good.
